specification.topic-statement: The key injection system buffers programmatically supplied byte sequences and feeds them into the REPL input pipeline one byte at a time, ahead of real keyboard input.

scope.in-scope: Buffer initialization, byte appending with dynamic growth, single-byte draining with auto-reset, pending-byte query, escape sequence unescaping, and event loop integration with priority over TTY input.
scope.boundary: Drained bytes are fed to the same stateful input parser that processes real keyboard bytes. The parsing of bytes into semantic actions (arrow keys, control characters, Unicode codepoints, etc.) is out of scope.

The control socket supplies raw key strings that are unescaped before being appended to the buffer. The socket protocol and command dispatch are out of scope; this specification covers behavior from the point at which unescaped bytes are available.

field.name: data
field.note: dynamically allocated; initial capacity 256 bytes; never shrinks

field.name: write position
field.note: byte offset of the next append; increases monotonically until drain completes

field.name: read position
field.note: byte offset of the next byte to drain

field.name: capacity
field.note: allocated size; doubles when an append would exceed it

behavior.buffer-initialization: The buffer is created with an initial capacity of 256 bytes. Both the write position and the read position start at zero.
behavior.byte-appending:
A byte sequence of a given length is appended to the buffer. If the current write position plus the incoming length would reach or exceed the current capacity, the capacity is doubled repeatedly until it is large enough. The bytes are then copied into the buffer starting at the write position, and the write position is advanced by the number of bytes appended.
The read position is not affected by an append.
Once the buffer grows, it does not release that capacity, even after the buffer is fully drained and reset.
behavior.byte-draining:
A single byte is removed from the front of the unread portion of the buffer and returned to the caller.
If the read position equals the write position (no unread bytes remain), the operation signals that no byte is available and returns without modifying any state.
If an unread byte is available, it is read from the current read position and the read position is advanced by one. If after this advance the read position equals the write position (the buffer has been fully drained), both positions are reset to zero, making the entire buffer capacity available for future appends.
The caller must call drain repeatedly to consume multiple bytes. This constraint is intentional — see event loop integration below.
behavior.pending-query: The number of unread bytes in the buffer is the write position minus the read position. This value is zero when the buffer is empty and positive when bytes are waiting to be drained.
behavior.escape-unescaping:
Before bytes are appended, a key string may be unescaped to convert C-style escape sequences into their raw byte equivalents. The input string is scanned left to right. Each recognized escape sequence is replaced with the corresponding byte:
`\r` produces a carriage return byte (0x0D). `\n` produces a linefeed byte (0x0A). `\t` produces a tab byte (0x09). `\\` produces a backslash byte (0x5C). `\xNN` where `NN` are two hexadecimal digits produces the byte with that numeric value.
Any backslash not followed by one of these recognized sequences is output as a literal backslash byte. The character following the backslash is processed normally on the next step.
All non-backslash characters are copied verbatim to the output.
behavior.event-loop-integration:
On each iteration of the main event loop, the injection buffer is checked for pending bytes before the event loop blocks on I/O.
If the buffer contains at least one unread byte, one byte is drained and fed to the input parser. The event loop then continues to the next iteration without waiting for I/O. This repeats until the buffer is empty.
Only when the buffer is empty does the event loop proceed to block on I/O, at which point real keyboard input may be read.
Only one byte is drained per event loop iteration, even if many bytes are pending. After each byte is processed, the frame is rendered and the loop restarts.
The input parser is stateful. A multi-byte escape sequence (such as an arrow key) must be delivered as consecutive bytes to the same parser invocations. If injected bytes were drained in bulk and then real TTY bytes arrived, the two streams could interleave inside an escape sequence, producing incorrect parse results. The one-byte-per-iteration design ensures injected bytes are fully consumed before any TTY bytes enter the parser.
Injected bytes are fed to the same stateful input parser instance that receives real keyboard bytes. From the parser's perspective, injected bytes are indistinguishable from bytes typed at the keyboard.
